coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) are the smallest nanomaterials (2–10 nm), with unique optical and electronic properties. Thanks to these properties, QDs have been standing during the last years as signal tags for different applications, including bioimaging, fluorescent biosensors and electrochemical assays. In this review, we explore the current state-of-the art on these nanomaterials, differentiating them between semiconductor and carbon-based QDs. Also, the review focuses on their unique advantages as transducers in different biosensing platforms.

head.ce:title: Quantum dots for biosensing: Classification and applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) are the smallest nanomaterials (2–10 nm), with unique optical and electronic properties. Thanks to these properties, QDs have been standing during the last years as signal tags for different applications, including bioimaging, fluorescent biosensors and electrochemical assays. In this review, we explore the current state-of-the art on these nanomaterials, differentiating them between semiconductor and carbon-based QDs. Also, the review focuses on their unique advantages as transducers in different biosensing platforms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, year 2024, one sitting at home watching a movie on a “QLED TV” may suddenly wonder “what is inside that screen?” After peeking a look on manufacturer's webpage (Haynes, 2021), it can be learned that the screen contains some nanomaterials named “quantum dots” (QDs, Lee et al., 2022). These nanoparticles are stored in different compartments of the liquid crystal display (LCD) and, when excited by a blue light emitting diode (LED), they emit red and green lights that, combined with the blue light of the LED, generate all the visible spectra of colors for us to enjoy our movie (Silva, 2023). As an interesting fact, a QLED TV is able to produce light with a brightness of 4000 nits (1 nit equals to 1 cd/m2), enough to overpower direct sunlight, being perfect for outdoor displays (Haynes, 2021).

Leaving aside TV facts, QDs (not to be confused with nanodots, which is a trademark) are nowadays considered the smallest nanoparticles, mostly ranged on a diameter between 2 and 10 nm. To have a clearer idea of their size, QDs are to a soccer ball what the soccer ball is to the Earth (Fig. 1 a, Reinhoudt, 2007). Being stricter with their definition, QDs are nanoscale semiconductors that can exhibit quantum (i.e. quantum, the smallest discrete unit of a phenomenon) mechanical properties thanks to their small size. Confined electrons on QDs result in quantum energy levels, differently from the continuous energy bands found in bulk semiconductors. This phenomenon leads to unique optical, electronic and, sometimes, magnetic properties (Alivisatos, 1996).

QDs are useful in a wide range of applications, including optoelectronic devices, solar cells, quantum computing or biological imaging, among others. In this review we focus on their application for biosensing, where QDs act as transducers (the elements that produce the signal).

In 1980–1981, Aleksey Yekimov (also known as Alexey I. Ekimov; Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2023 for his contribution to the discovery and synthesis of QDs, Fernholm et al., 2023) characterized size-dependent quantum effects in colored glass. These color changes came from CuCl nanoparticles and Yekimov demonstrated that the color was dependent on the particle size (Ekimov and Onushchenko, 1980; Ekimov, 1981). At the same time, Louis E. Brus (also sharing the Chemistry Nobel Prize, 2023; Fernholm et al., 2023) observed that similar quantum effects were present in CdS particles, being the smaller particles emitting a more blue-shifted light when excited than larger particles (Rossetti et al., 1983; Brus, 1984). It was not until 1993 that QDs were synthetized with reproducibility, smooth surfaces and well-controlled diameter, by Moungi G. Bawendi (sharing a third of the Chemistry Nobel Prize, 2023; Fernholm et al., 2023). Bawendi's synthetic method was easy and revolutionary, producing CdS, CdSe and CdTe QDs from 1.2 to 11.5 nm based on the pyrolysis of organometallic reagents by injection into a hot coordinating solvent, providing quantum nucleation phenomena and separating the QDs by size selective controlled precipitation (Murray et al., 1993).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electronic properties of quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: When QDs are excited by UV light, some electrons get sufficient energy to move from the valence band (i.e. the shallower orbital in the atom) to a conductance band, generated around the nanoparticle. Although confined to the QD, electrons can move freely and can conduct electricity around the conductance band. After the excitation, when the electrons return to the holes left on the valence band, photons are emitted. The color that we can observe from this emission depends on the energy gap between the conductance and the valence band (Klimov, 2007; Mittelstädt et al., 2022; Agarwal et al., 2023). Hereby, this energy gap is dependent on QDs size. Smaller QDs produce higher energy gaps, bluer tones (e.g. 510 nm emission wavelength, around 3 nm QDs), while larger QDs generate lower energy differences, related to redder glows (e.g. 610 nm emission wavelength, around 8 nm QDs) (Fig. 1b–Murray et al., 1993; Medintz et al., 2005; Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Pisanic II et al., 2014). This size dependence is what differentiates QDs from bulk materials, in which the energy gap depends only on the properties of the material. Fig. 1c illustrates this gap dependance, inversely proportional to the QD size. In QDs, the excess energy dissipation coupled with bandgap-determined exciton decay causes the photon emission (Thurner and Debbage, 2018; Agarwal et al., 2023). Note that QDs do not follow Kasha's Rule, which states that fluorescence yield and energy emission will always be independend of the excitation wavelenght (Kasha, 1950), since QDs require a specific wavelength to be excited (Buranda et al., 2011; Cushing et al., 2013; Debnath et al., 2023).

Besides these optical properties, the conductance band is also responsible for the existing semiconductor and electrocatalytic properties of QDs (Karadurmus et al., 2021), although in many cases an extra shell of a different material is required to improve the QDs conductance and stability (“core shell QDs”, CSQDs; Sahu and Kumar, 2022), since they can be degraded in time by the accumulation of electrons (Gao et al., 2024). Also, the magnetic properties of QDs are enhanced in comparison to their nanoparticle counterpart, while lacking magnetic hysteresis effect (i.e. when the magnetic field is not being applied, QDs do not continue magnetized; Katwal, 2023). However, due their small size, their movement within a magnetic field is slow, so often QDs are coupled on magnetic micro/nanoparticles, acting as carriers, being the resulting hybrid nanomaterial called “magnetic QDs” (MQDs), although the dimension of it is larger than 10 nm (often, hundreds of nanometers; Munasinghe et al., 2020; Gupta and Sharma, 2021; Ma et al., 2021; Gidwani et al., 2023).

A biosensor is an analytical device combining a biological element, responsible for the sensing, and a transducer, responsible for the signal production (Turner, 2013). Biosensors have a wide range of applications, including point-of-care (PoC; i.e. using the device for diagnostics where the patient is located, in a fast mode and in real time; Quesada-González and Merkoçi, 2018), environmental sensing (note that a biosensor is not limited to detecting biological substances; Kurniawan et al., 2022), and medical imaging (Gil et al., 2021; Abdellatif et al., 2022). When developing a biosensor the key points to aim for are high robustness and scalability (i.e. the device is low dependent of factors such as weather or user performance, and it is easy and cost-efficient to fabricate with a high reproducibility level), low limits of detection (LoD; i.e. it can identify the target analyte at low concentration levels) and high sensitivity (i.e. the signal intensities of two close values of concentration are sufficiently differentiable).

Using QDs as transducers on biosensors may help achieving most of these key points (Bruno, 2022), since their production is scalable and therefore robust (they are being applied on TV and computer industry), and costs are not much higher than other nanomaterials. In addition, fluorescent labels, such as QDs, are known for usually being more sensitive and exhibit lower detection limits than colorimetric labels (e.g. gold nanoparticles, AuNPs; Bai et al., 2012) and, as an extra feature, they can be used for multiplexing due their different emission wavelengths, based on QDs size, in the presence of a single source of blue or ultraviolet light (Sena-Torralba et al., 2022a). However, not all QDs are stable and easily tend to aggregate, especially in certain buffers, losing their fluorescent properties. Fluorescence can be dissipated or completely turned off as well in certain biological matrixes. And, furthermore, most of the QDs present toxicity issues which make them non-viable for in vivo applications (Nikazar et al., 2020; Gidwani et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of quantum dots and common synthesis methods
ce:section.ce:para: There exist different terminologies to classify QDs, however the scientific community has not reached a clear agreement (Park et al., 2021). Some classifications consider clusters and nanoclusters as QDs, but in the humble opinion of the authors these definitions are more intended for groups of metallic nuclei (which could be, or not, groups of QDs) grouped stably and close together by means of different types of bonds or interactions (Wong et al., 2021a, 2021b). In this review we are following the classification proposed by Cayuela et al. (2016), distinguishing between semiconductor QDs (SQDs; those based on metallic and other inorganic elements) and carbon-based QDs (CQDs; including carbon-based elements such as graphene).

SQDs are composed of inorganic elements forming a crystalline structure and, mainly, spherical shape (Table 1 ). Depending on their composition, they can be soluble in water (hydrophilic) or in organic solvents (hydrophobic). SQDs containing heavy metals are probably the most cytotoxic type of QDs. On the other hand, SQDs made of other inorganic elements such as silicon (a metalloid), could be less toxic and biocompatible, but their harmfulness is still under discussion (Morozova et al., 2020; Beri, 2023).

SQDs are mainly produced by “bottom-up” methods, in solution the nuclei are generated and atoms are assembled around growing the nanostructure. One of the most common synthetic procedures is the “hot injection method” (Fig. 2 a), which involves injecting a solution containing the metal precursor and anions into a hot solvent containing surfactants and ligands to stabilize and determine the growth of the SQDs (Liu et al., 2021). The “heat-up method” is a simplified version of this procedure, removing the injection step, being ligands and precursors heated together in one pot (Dias et al., 2017). This second method is easier to perform in the laboratory, but the obtained SQDs will have a wider size distribution than by hot injection. Another advantage of this method is that it can be performed even using a microwave (Washington II and Strouse, 2008; Moghaddam et al., 2012). A third alternative is the “cluster-assisted method” in which small organometallic clusters help the nucleation of semiconductor precursors in the presence of a coordinating solvent (Fig. 2b; Jawaid et al., 2013). Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is another method, involving the controlled deposition of precursor gases onto a substrate, where they undergo chemical reactions. This method enables precise control over the size and composition of QDs (Reilly et al., 2021). All these methods have a main drawback, they are non-scalable. In industry, the “continuous-flow method” is employed, involving reactors mixing two different phases, which could be both liquid or one of them liquid and the other gas (Yen et al., 2005). The method has demonstrated size monodispersity and can be adapted even to microfluidic reactors (Fig. 2c; Pan et al., 2013).

“Top-down” methods (involving the breakdown of a material through external forces) are mainly employed when QDs are intended for optoelectronics and quantum computing production (Chen et al., 2023a), therefore, we will not enter in detail within this review. These methods include techniques such as molecular beam epitaxy (compound sources are heated in high vacuum producing a molecular or atomic beam that can condense on a substrate producing the QDs; Li et al., 2023), and electron-beam and X-ray lithographies (a beam is used to pattern the QD structure on a resist material; Ko et al., 2023).

The main methods to functionalize SQDs with biomolecules (e.g. DNA, aptamers) are based on covalent interactions (e.g. thiols, amides, esters; Mitchell et al., 1999; Rosi and Mirkin, 2005). Current functionalization methods are so precise that permit assembling SQDs in dimers, arrays and other complex structures (Zhang and Liedl, 2019; Chen et al., 2023b).
There exist more complex SQDs structures, composed of more than one type of material. These SQDs can be classified in two subcategories.

Core shell SQDs (CSQDs) are those in which a SQD is surrounded by one or several layers of other inorganic elements (Table 1). To stabilize and increase the life of SQDs these are often encapsulated in organic surfactants, however it may reduce the fluorescence yield of the nanoparticle. On the contrary, CSQDs do not only stabilize the QDs with an inorganic material layer but increase their photoluminescence efficiency while protecting them from photo-oxidation (Vasudevan et al., 2015). The resulting CSQDs properties are often a sum of the different layers composing the nanomaterial. Their biofunctionalization can be carried out by many different methods, including covalent bonding, electrostatic forces, use of chelators and passive adsorption (Gao et al., 2005).

The most common synthetic procedure is the “ionic layer adsorption and reaction method”, which consists of growing layers on a purified solution of SQDs. The volume and concentration of each injection determines the thickness of each layer (Linkova et al., 2019; Kumara et al., 2022).

Alloyed SQDs (ASQDs) contain gradient internal structures (Table 1), with different cation-cation, cation-anion or anion-anion ratios which determine the properties of the resulting QDs (Susumu et al., 2017; Sahu et al., 2023). ASDQs add extra possibilities to engineer the conductance band gap, in addition to their size dependence, which allows them to emit at bright blue and near infrared spectra. CSQDs can be transformed into ASQDs by high-temperature annealing reactions or by recrystallization processes (Samokhvalov et al., 2013).

CQDs are those made of carbon derivates. Depending on how carbon atoms are arranged, CQDs can be classified in three subcategories: amorphous CQDs (ACQDs), spherical CQDs (known as simply “carbon dots”; CDs) and graphene QDs (GQDs; Kurniawan et al., 2022; Cayuela et al., 2016). In all the cases, they are water-soluble and their toxicity is expected lower than SQDs.

The synthetic methods for CQDs are very diverse, based in both bottom-up (e.g. starting with citric acid or glucose as precursors; Boukhvalov and Osipov, 2023; Shi et al., 2023) and top-down (transforming a bulk material into quantum particles, e.g. liquid exfoliation of coal; Kurniawan et al., 2022; Bruno et al., 2021) approaches. Hydrothermal (in water medium) and solvothermal (in organic solvents) syntheses are the most common procedures, both following bottom-up strategies, consisting of treating the precursors at high pressures above the boiling point in autoclave. Note that both can be considered “green methods”, involving the use of water or biodegradable solvents (such as ethanol) as the medium for the synthetic procedure, offering cost-effective and sustainable approaches, often minimizing the use of toxic chemicals and highly energetic processes (Fang et al., 2024). Furthermore, with these methods CQDs can be doped in the presence of nitrogen to enhance their fluorescent properties (Monday et al., 2021; Chang et al., 2022). Other considered green methods would be laser ablation (a physical approach to break larger particles into smaller ones; Shorgar et al., 2023) and bacterial synthesis (bacteria are exposed to carbon-containing precursors which are reduced to CQDs; Sahu and Sahoo, 2024).

CVD has also been reported as synthetic method for CQDs (Yan et al., 2016), and although the method may offer a better control over shape and dimensions of the CQDs compared to other bottom-up methods, conditions such as vacuum and high temperatures might make it more complex and expensive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amorphous carbon-based quantum dots and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: ACQDs consist of carbon and 5–50 % oxygen. The final shape is quite spherical (Table 1) and, for their functionalization, normally it is performed during their synthesis. CDs are composed of graphitic layers of sp2 C, attracted by π-π stacking ending in a spherical shape (Table 1). They can be conjugated with biomolecules thanks to their π-π stacking interactions, hydrogen bonds and covalent reactions. Modifying CDs with OH terminated polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are one of the most known methods to enhance the fluorescence yield (green emission) of these QDs (Nsibande and Forbes, 2020; Minervini et al., 2022).

GQDs consist of a single layer of graphene of dimensions lower than 10 nm. Its shape is, therefore, planar (Table 1). The properties and conjugation capabilities of GQD are alike CDs, but exhibit properties of single-layer graphene, such as increased thermal conductivity. GQDs could be composed of graphene oxide, exhibiting in this form the “giant red-edge effect”. This phenomenon implies a big shift toward longer wavelength (i.e. the red end of the light spectrum), making the QDs look redder than expected. It can happen due to environmental conditions, such as changing the media to a polar solvent (Cushing et al., 2013, Cushing et al., 2017; Gan et al., 2016).

Fluorescent methods are known to provide a more intense yield than colorimetric (naked-eye) methods, so QDs stand as preferred transducer in fluorescent-based biosensors. In comparison to fluorescent dyes, often attached on antibodies, QDs are more stable in time, emit fluorescence for longer periods and, in some cases, transportable at room temperature. Also, the fluorescent yield is greater in QDs (Morales-Narvaez et al., 2012).

In many examples of biosensors, “quenching phenomena” is employed. Quenching is the decrease or loss of fluorescence, which is result of a variety of molecular interactions, normally happening when the quencher element is in close contact to the transducer. In QDs, quenching phenomena can be due static (quencher and QD form a non-fluorescent complex) or dynamic (energy is transferred between the QD and the quencher) processes, “inner filter effect” (overlapping of emission and absorption spectra of QDs which is provoked by the quencher) or “Förster resonance energy transfer” (FRET; the emission spectra of the QDs is absorbed by the absorption spectra of the quencher; Yang et al., 2019). For example, in the work of Zor et al. (2015) they use the quenching effect of GQDs, combined with magnetic beads, to detect a pesticide molecule which quenches GQDs when in contact. Therefore, the signal of the system decreases as higher is the concentration of the analyte. Yang et al. (2021) demonstrated that CSQDs can be encapsulated within a 3D network imprinted polymer and yet emit fluorescent signal, being quenched in presence of ascorbic acid, a very typical quencher agent (of interest in biosensing since it is involved in many biological processes). This method could be very efficient to filter the samples using the magnetic beads, but intended to be used only in the lab and not on the field, since the method would require several steps and dedicated equipment (therefore, also trained personnel).

Microarrays are very common in diagnostics, with low volumes of sample, transducer and biological recognition element it is possible to multiplex, analyze several targets at the same time. In 2012, Morales-Narvaez et al. (2012) demonstrated that QDs not only were applicable in microarrays, but that the fluorescence yield was greater than Alexa 647 dye, commonly used in these assays. Years later, in the same group, Cheeveewattanagul et al. created a paper-based biosensor for Escherichia coli bacteria detection immobilizing graphene and antibody-labelled QDs on the paper substrate. In presence of the target, QDs were separated from the quencher, the graphene, and thus activating the light emission on paper (Fig. 3 a), always under the appropriate excitation source. Similar works have been later developed by immobilizing QDs on paper, using wax to limit the sampling area (similar to a 96-well plate; Zong et al., 2019), being able to even record the fluorescence signal using a smartphone (Álvarez-Diduk et al., 2017). These are first examples of QDs sensing methods adapted to be used in the field, although a device for measuring the response will always be needed (which could simply be a smartphone; Quesada-González and Merkoçi, 2017).

Among the different types of paper-based biosensors, lateral flow assays (LFAs; Quesada-González and Merkoçi, 2015; Quesada-González, 2018; Sena-Torralba et al., 2022b; Quesada-González and Merkoçi, 2023) are highlighted due to their simplicity of use, fast response and versatility to detect different types of analytes while using different types of sensing strategies. LFAs are strongly stablished in medical environment (known as “rapid diagnostic tests”; Rubio-Monterde et al., 2023), commonly used for PoC applications (Quesada-González and Merkoçi, 2018; despite they can also be used for environmental sensing; Quesada-González et al., 2018) and had an important role during SARS-CoV-2 outbreak in 2020 (Merkoçi et al., 2021). Several LFAs using QDs have been reported (Bai et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2021), and in these devices it is even possible to quantify the response. Normally, LFAs provide a qualitative naked-eye response, yes/no (two lines/one line), but the intensity of test line is proportional to the quantity of nanoparticles (or QDs) which stop on it (when the target is present, being captured by two bioreceptors, one on the paper substrate, nitrocellulose, and the other on the transducer). In Bai et al., 2012)'s work, they compared standard AuNPs-based LFAs against QDs-based LFAs, realizing that the second was not only able to reach a lower LoD, but the sensitivity was also greater (it was easier to differentiate intensities related to close concentration values). In low concentration samples, the color of AuNPs can be negligible for the human eye, but for the same amount of QDs, the fluorescent signal can be detected. Of course, this method implies a clear drawback if compared to using colorimetric labels, the user needs a source of light, a UV-lamp in the referenced examples, to excite the QDs. Nevertheless, commercial and portable fluorescent readers for LFAs are available (Qiagen; Qassay; DETECKT).

The previously mentioned strategy for detecting bacteria by cancelling the quenching of QDs in contact with graphene (when the bacteria separate both elements; Cheeveewattanagul et al., 2017) can be applied on LFAs, as demonstrated by Merkoçi's group (Morales-Narváez et al., 2015). QDs are dispensed on nitrocellulose paper, as test and control lines (conjugated with antibodies that can recognize proteins from the target bacteria), then the bacteria are added to the strip and later a graphene solution. If there is no target, graphene will quench all the QDs, but in presence of bacteria the QDs on test line will remain fluorescent (while control line will be turned-off), since the bacteria will generate a gap between QDs and graphene. Variations and applications of this strategy have been later reported (Zamora-Gálvez et al., 2018; Hassan et al., 2019). The strategy, although seems to exhibit improved sensitivity levels, implies additional steps that a non-trained user would struggle to follow, which is against the simplicity philosophy of LFAs, often single step and user-friendly platforms.

Sena-Torralba et al. (2022a), presented another interesting ratiometric approach using QD on a LFA platform. Blue-light emitting QDs were dispensed on test line, while the transducer of the system was red-light emitting QDs. If the sample is positive, more red-light emitting QDs are combined with the blue-light emitting QDs and therefore the provided signal is purple-colored (Fig. 3b). In absence of analyte, the test line emits only blue light. In this way, the ratio of the color indicates the concentration of protein on the paper strip. If no QDs are placed on the test line, the sensitivity of the system is greater (it is more sensitive to measure emission intensity against its absence than a color chance), however this proof-of-concept application is very interesting, combining QDs emitting at several wavelengths, different analytes could be evaluated on just a single spot (similar to QLED-TV principle).

Qin et al. (2022) proposed an array for detecting eleven different types of heavy metals using CDs (Fig. 3c). In their system, they use a single type of CDs so, indifferently of the heavy metal being detected, the maximum emission peak is always located on the same wavelength. However, they conjugate the CDs with six different amino acids, the proportion of recognition of each target being different depending on the amino acid. Thus, they can differentiate each heavy metal by comparing the emission intensities of the six amino acid-CDs conjugates.

Biological imaging (or bioimaging) is a method to visualize biological functions in real time avoiding using tools that penetrate the skin or physically invade the body of the specimen (human or animal) while the transducer is producing the signal. Although, at the moment bioimaging applying QDs is not a technique being used in hospitals due the high toxicity of these materials (Fig. 4 a, proposal by Abdellatif et al. on how medical imaging using QDs could work in the future), there exist some proof-of-concept reports and proposals (Gil et al., 2021; Abdellatif et al., 2022; Wegner and Hildebrandt, 2015; Pandey and Bodas, 2020).

As mentioned before, QDs can be used for multiplexing by taking advantage of the different wavelength emissions that can be produced, when using different QDs together, at the same excitation wavelength. This strategy was employed by Liu et al. (2010) to label different types of cells with different QDs and then differentiate them on an inverted fluorescence microscope. To highlight that fluorescence lifetime of QDs (over 10 ns) is longer than that of organic dyes (less than 5 ns), as it was studied by Liang et al. (2015). They explored these lifetimes of QDs in rat liver, in vivo, using a multiphoton microscope adapted for fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIM). In Fig. 4b it is observed how QDs remain stable, capable of emitting fluorescence, inside the rat liver even after 2h.

Other cases of bioimaging involve clusters of QDs (Du et al., 2017), CDs-based MQDs (i.e. CDs on magnetic carriers; Pakkath et al., 2018) and CQDs attached onto nanozymes (i.e. nanomaterials mimicking an enzymatic activity; Wang et al., 2020). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a reconstruction of a magnetic resonance field to generate an image, measured by large magnet and radio waves. MRI is considered a bioimaging technique and Pakkath et al. (2018) demonstrated that MQDs can provide signals of intensity comparable to MRI medical contrast agents.

Confined electrons on QDs conductance band grant them potential to act as electrocatalysts (i.e. they can participate and accelerate or favor electrochemical reactions) and that activity can be measured. High surface-to-volume ratio on QDs implies a large number of surface atoms available for activating different reactants, a key feature for electrocatalysis (Al-Sheikhi and Al-Abedi, 2021). Also, the governance of quantum effects leads to discrete energy levels, optimizing energy alignment of the reaction intermediates (Klimov et al., 2000). The electrochemical methods to detect QDs are diverse, depending on what is applied, whether potential or current, and what is measured, impedance, potential, current, etc. (Karadurmus et al., 2021).

For example, Marín et al. (2011) measured the oxidation of Cd0 to Cd2+ in the surface of CdS SQDs on screen printed electrodes (Fig. 5 a) using square wave voltammetry. Medina-Sánchez et al. (2014) used QDs as electrochemical labels to detect apolipoprotein E (related to Alzheimer disease) by means of square wave voltammetry measurements in screen printed electrodes. Their system was adapted into a microfluidic platform that included a preconcentration step involving magnetic beads. However, for detecting QDs with this method, these needed to be dissolved in acid buffer (pH 3 acetate buffer in this case), to release metallic cations that are later reoxidized in the working electrode. So, despite the microfluidic platform, the system required several steps and conditions, and not really taking advantage of the QDs electrocatalytical properties. Roushania et al. (2018) took advantage of the conductive properties of GQDs to create an aptamer-based electrochemical sensor on glassy carbon electrodes. On the electrode, AuNPs were deposited and, using thiol groups, GQDs were connected to gold, at the same time that the GQD, by means of amino groups, were connected to silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). These AgNPs were conjugated with an aptamer which shape, only in presence of the analyte, was altered. That aptamer folding, taking place near the electrode surface, can be detected by impedance spectroscopy.

Electrocatalytic properties of QDs still need to be studied for their application on electrochemical sensors, since in most cases they need to be treated with acids (e.g. HNO3 or HCl) to release the metallic cations which are later oxidized and measured (contrary to some nanomaterials, e.g. IrO2 nanoparticles, Quesada-González et al., 2019, which can catalyze reactions at neutral pH avoiding the need to decompose them during the reaction). Nevertheless, QDs still offer the same advantage as the other biosensing strategies previously mentioned, multiplexing. The differentiable electrocatalytic properties of this nanomaterial (or composition, in case the QDs are being dissolved) allow using and differentiate multiple transducers at the same time (providing different signaling peaks; Kokkinos et al., 2016).

Jiang et al. (2022) produced planar SQDs from MXenes (“M” stands for a transition metal, “X” for carbon or nitrogen; Karadurmus et al., 2023), a 2D-nanomaterial that consist of thin layers of transition metal carbides, nitrides or carbonitrides, with properties reminiscent of those of graphene. These SQDs exhibit high electrochemiluminescence quantum efficiency. The authors synthetized their SQDs by a top-down method from the MXenes, and then immobilized them on AgPt nanoparticles (Fig. 5b). The sensor was based on an immunosandwich assay, represented in Fig. 5c. According to the author's theory, the SQDs catalyze peroxydisulfates reduction and then these reduced species and the electrode are responsible for activating the energy gap on the QDs conductance band (contrary to photoluminescence, where it is activated by photoexcitation). Thus, if QDs are excited by current, the electrocatalytic reaction is enhanced (Fig. 5d).

Electrochemiluminescent techniques often depend on the concentration of secondary species to perform the reaction; on contrast, photoelectrochemical reactions require no additives and present lower background signal and usually higher sensitivity (Cheng et al., 2023). SQDs can absorb light and generate electron-hole pairs which can participate in redox reactions, therefore the light energy can enhance the efficiency of electron transfer and different catalytic processes. However, the literature on QDs photoelectrochemical-based biosensing is still scarce, being QDs photoelectrochemical applications more oriented to solar cells and non-biological sensors. A standard photoelectrochemical cell requires an auxiliar and a working electrode, being the second one usually a semiconductor, a role which QDs can take care of (Hlaváčová and Skládal, 2024; Cheng et al., 2023, report a photoelectrochemical biosensor combining DNA on QDs and exploiting the quenching effect of those. Hlaváčová and Skládal, 2024, instead opt for enzymatic-based reactions catalyzed by a QD covered electrode. The immobilization of the QDs on the electrode surface result in an extended lifetime of the electron–hole pair, provoking a photoelectrochemical current increase.

QDs, in regard to their optical properties, are primarily chosen for their fluorescent characteristics; however, there are few reports on their use in colorimetric biosensing (Li et al., 2021; Yue et al., 2024). As an example, Li and coworkers incorporated CdSe/ZnS ASQDs on a paper substrate to detect Hg2+ cations. The CdSe/ZnS ASQDs can catalize 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation in presence of Hg2+, thus providing a visible change of colour (from no color visible to blue) detectable by naked eye. Colorimetric methods might be less sensitive than the fluorescent ones, however their simplicity and no need of equipment for response reading could grant them a strong position when in situ and fast applications are needed.

MQDs have potential for being applied in magnetism-based biosensors, as we previously exposed, QDs enhance the magnetic properties of the magnetic beads and have already been applied on bioimaging (Katwal, 2023; Pakkath et al., 2018). QDs also have potential as Raman reporters (Raman spectroscopy detects low frequency phenomena, such as atoms vibration, rotation and inelastic movements of atoms), especially GQDs (Dervishi et al., 2019; Panyathip et al., 2021). To highlight that Surface-enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) is a non-destructive method, extending the lifetime of the probe (Song et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Yosri et al., 2025). Also, biosensing based on QDs thermal conductivity should be feasible, few recent reports demonstrate that quantum temperature measurements are indeed possible (Petrini et al., 2020; Annby-Andersson et al., 2020; Bhandari et al., 2023).

There is no nanomaterial as bright as QDs. This property of QDs, not only superior in intensity but in emission lifetime, makes them stand as transducers in many fluorescent-based biosensing and biological imaging applications. In addition, their small size is a “double edge sword”. In one hand, a large number of QDs can be carried on bigger particles (as more QDs can be loaded per particle, greater the signal). Also, small particles can enter easily on cells or bacteria, for imaging. On the other hand, precisely because they can easily enter cells, their cytotoxicity is high, making their application difficult for medical and diagnostic purposes. However, for in vitro diagnostic tests, such as LFAs, since the nanomaterial is never in contact with the user, the use of QDs should not lead to concerns (LFAs used in medical facilities are discarded as biohazard waste, indifferently of the components of the strip).

Trends on other biosensing methods, those non-based on fluorescence, are still to be developed or to be improved. There exist several electrochemical-based sensors, but these mainly focus on detecting torn off metallic ions from the QDs, rather than measuring quantum energy transfers or catalyzed reactions, with few exceptions.

A very clear advantage of QDs is their potential for multiplexing. Mixing different sized QDs and exciting them at the same wavelength but receiving a different and distinguishable signal for each of them is their strongest capability.
